title: What Is Sacred Tradition?
excerpt: A concise look at how Sacred Scripture and Sacred Tradition work together in Catholic theology.
body: Catholic theology does not treat Sacred Tradition as a rival to Scripture. Tradition is the living transmission of the Gospel in the Church, while Scripture is the inspired written witness to that same Gospel.
The Catechism explains that Scripture, Tradition, and the Magisterium are bound together and cannot stand independently. This preserves doctrinal continuity across centuries.
Practical takeaway: when reading disputed passages, Catholics read with the Church across time, not as isolated interpreters.

title: The Eucharist in John 6
excerpt: How the Bread of Life discourse informs Catholic belief in the Real Presence.
body: In John 6, Jesus shifts from metaphorical language to insistence: His flesh is true food and His blood is true drink.
Many disciples leave because the saying is hard, and Jesus does not soften the claim. Catholic interpretation sees this as a key biblical witness to Eucharistic realism.
This does not stand alone. The Last Supper narratives and Pauline witness in 1 Corinthians reinforce the same sacramental pattern.

title: Why Catholics Pray with the Psalms
excerpt: The Psalms shape Catholic liturgy, personal prayer, and theological imagination.
body: The Psalms are the prayerbook of Israel and of the Church. They train the believer to speak to God with honesty, trust, repentance, and praise.
In the Liturgy of the Hours, the Church sanctifies time by praying the Psalms throughout the day. The language of lament and hope forms spiritual resilience.
For beginners, Psalm 23 and Psalm 51 provide a strong entry point into scriptural prayer.

title: Faith and Works: James and Paul
excerpt: A Catholic reading of justification that avoids false oppositions.
body: Paul rejects works of the Mosaic law as the basis of salvation, while James rejects a dead faith without charity. Catholic theology reads both as complementary, not contradictory.
Grace initiates and sustains salvation. Human cooperation, enabled by grace, is real and meaningful.
This synthesis protects the primacy of grace while preserving the moral seriousness of discipleship.

title: Mary in Salvation History
excerpt: Why Marian doctrine is Christ-centered and rooted in Scripture.
body: Catholic teaching on Mary emerges from reflection on Christ. Titles like Theotokos protect orthodox Christology by affirming that the one born of Mary is truly God incarnate.
Biblical typology links Mary with the Ark of the Covenant and with Eve as the new woman in redemption.
Authentic Marian devotion always leads to deeper communion with Jesus and the life of the Church.
